content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:75499fd8

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains several durable user/pet facts and preferences plus useful contextual rationale around dog care, toy choice, medication handling, and collar selection. Forced memory write requires non-noop.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace user; no existing user entity is available.
- mention=Max; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PERSON; name=Max; reason=Max is a concrete recurring pet mentioned across turns; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Kong Toy; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Kong Toy; reason=Concrete product chosen for Max; no existing entity is available.
- mention=Yellow Dog Design; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Yellow Dog Design; reason=Named brand recommended and selected; no existing entity is available.
- mention=nylon collar from Yellow Dog Design; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=Yellow Dog Design nylon collar for Max; reason=User states intent to get this concrete personal item for Max; later claims may need it as an object.
- mention=engraved name tag; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=Engraved name tag for Max; reason=User states intent to get a concrete name tag to go with Max's collar; later claims may need it as an object.
- mention=Golden Retriever; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Golden Retriever; reason=Dog breed is a reusable concept relevant to Max.
- mention=peanut butter; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=Peanut butter; reason=Concrete food item Max likes and intended for use with Kong Toy; no existing entity is available.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Max|Kong Toy|Yellow Dog Design|Golden Retriever
        text_queries=Max dog flea and tick prevention medication every two weeks|Max loves peanut butter Kong Toy interactive dog toy|Max Yellow Dog Design nylon collar engraved name tag active lifestyle|Max Golden Retriever
        predicate_hints=owns|prefers|has_constraint|current_metric_value

Session date: 2023/05/22 (Mon) 10:50
Transcript:
user: I need help finding a good dog walker in my area. Do you have any recommendations or a list of services that can connect me with a reliable walker?
assistant: Finding a trustworthy dog walker can be a daunting task! Don't worry, I've got you covered. Here are some recommendations and services that can connect you with a reliable dog walker in your area:

**Online Platforms:**

1. **Rover**: Rover is one of the most popular platforms that connects dog owners with local dog walkers and sitters. You can browse profiles, read reviews, and book a walker that fits your needs.
2. **Care.com**: While primarily a childcare platform, Care.com also offers pet care services, including dog walking. You can search for walkers in your area and view their profiles.
3. **Wag!**: Wag! is a dog walking and dog sitting platform that connects you with local walkers. You can book a 30-minute or 1-hour walk, and even track your dog's route in real-time.
4. ** Barkly Pets**: Barkly Pets is a platform that connects dog owners with local dog walkers and sitters. You can browse profiles, read reviews, and book a walker that fits your needs.

**National Dog Walking Services:**

1. **Pet Sitters International**: Pet Sitters International is a network of professional pet sitters and dog walkers across the US. You can search for a walker in your area and contact them directly.
2. **National Association of Professional Pet Sitters**: The NAPPS is a professional organization that connects pet owners with local pet sitters and dog walkers. You can search for a walker in your area and view their profiles.

**Local Referrals:**

1. **Ask Friends, Family, or Neighbors**: Ask people you trust if they know of any reliable dog walkers in your area.
2. **Local Pet Stores or Veterinarians**: Visit local pet stores or veterinarians and ask if they know of any dog walkers they can recommend.
3. **Community Facebook Groups**: Join local Facebook groups or online forums and ask for dog walker recommendations.

**Tips for Finding a Reliable Dog Walker:**

1. **Check Reviews**: Always read reviews from multiple sources to get a sense of the walker's reliability and quality of service.
2. **Conduct an Interview**: Before booking a walker, conduct an interview to discuss your dog's needs, the walker's experience, and their approach to dog walking.
3. **Verify Insurance and Certifications**: Make sure the walker has liability insurance and any relevant certifications (e.g., pet first aid).
4. **Get a Meet-and-Greet**: Meet the walker in person with your dog to ensure they're a good fit.

Remember to always prioritize your dog's safety and well-being when searching for a dog walker. Good luck!
user: I will definitely check out those platforms. Do you think a dog walker would be able to handle Max's flea and tick prevention medication application every two weeks?
assistant: It's great that you're thinking about Max's flea and tick prevention medication. While dog walkers can provide exercise and companionship, administering medication might not be a standard service they offer. However, it's definitely worth asking!

Some dog walkers might be willing to administer flea and tick prevention medication as an additional service, especially if they have experience with dog care or have worked with dogs with similar needs. Others might not feel comfortable or be trained to do so.

Here are a few factors to consider when asking a dog walker about medication administration:

1. **Experience:** Does the dog walker have experience with administering medication to dogs? Have they worked with dogs that require regular medication?
2. **Certifications:** Are they certified in pet first aid or CPR? While not directly related to medication administration, these certifications demonstrate a level of expertise in dog care.
3. **Comfort level:** Is the dog walker comfortable administering medication, and are they willing to take on this responsibility?
4. **Liability:** Who would be liable in case of an adverse reaction or incorrect administration of medication?

If you find a dog walker who is willing to administer Max's flea and tick prevention medication, make sure to:

1. **Provide clear instructions:** Leave detailed instructions on the medication, including dosage, frequency, and any potential side effects.
2. **Leave a schedule:** Create a schedule or calendar to ensure the walker knows when to apply the medication.
3. **Monitor and follow up:** Regularly check in with the walker to confirm the medication has been administered correctly and on schedule.

Alternatively, you could consider other options for medication administration, such as:

1. **Hiring a pet sitter:** Pet sitters often provide more comprehensive care, including medication administration, feeding, and overnight stays.
2. **Asking a trusted neighbor or friend:** If you have a trusted neighbor or friend who is familiar with Max, you could ask them to help with medication administration.
3. **Automated medication dispensers:** There are automated medication dispensers available that can be programmed to dispense medication at set times.

Remember to always prioritize Max's health and safety, and don't hesitate to ask questions or seek guidance from your veterinarian if you're unsure about medication administration.
user: I'm also thinking of getting Max a new toy, something that can keep him engaged during the day while I'm away. Do you have any recommendations for interactive dog toys?
assistant: What a great idea! Interactive dog toys can be a wonderful way to keep Max engaged, stimulated, and happy while you're away. Here are some fantastic options that are sure to delight Max:

1. **Kong Toy**: The Kong is a classic interactive toy that can be stuffed with treats, peanut butter, or even canned dog food. The hollow center provides a challenge for Max to figure out how to get the treats out.
2. **Tricky Treat Ball**: This treat-dispensing ball requires Max to roll it around to release treats. The adjustable difficulty level and varied treat sizes keep the game exciting.
3. **Puzzle Toys**: Puzzle toys, like the Nina Ottosson Dog Brick or the Outward Hound Brick Puzzle, challenge Max to figure out how to get the treats out by sliding, lifting, or rotating the pieces.
4. **Interactive Feeders**: Interactive feeders, such as the Outward Hound Sensory Maze or the Pet Zone Treat Wobbler, provide a fun and challenging way for Max to eat his meals.
5. **Hide-a-Squirrel**: This plush toy has hidden compartments and squeakers, encouraging Max to use his problem-solving skills to find the hidden treats.
6. **West Paw Design's Wobbler**: This weighted, treat-dispensing toy wobbles and moves, making it a fun and challenging way for Max to get his treats.
7. **Sensory Mats**: Sensory mats, like the Snuffle Mat or the Pawfect Pets Sensory Mat, provide a fun and calming way for Max to engage his sense of smell and explore.
8. **Tug Toys with Treats**: Tug toys with hidden treats, such as the Tug-A-Jug or the Ever Pet Interactive Tug Toy, encourage Max to use his problem-solving skills to get the treats out.
9. **Electronic Interactive Toys**: Electronic toys, like the Petcube Bites or the Furbo Interactive Dog Camera, allow you to interact with Max remotely, dispensing treats and playing with him even when you're not there.
10. **DIY Interactive Toys**: You can also create your own interactive toys using household items, such as cardboard boxes, paper bags, and treats. Get creative and have fun!

Remember to choose a toy that suits Max's age, breed, and individual needs. Introduce new toys gradually to prevent overwhelming him, and always supervise playtime to ensure his safety.

Which one of these toys do you think Max would love the most?
user: I think Max would love the Kong Toy. I already know he loves peanut butter, and it would be great to stuff the Kong with peanut butter-filled Kongs as a special treat.
assistant: The Kong Toy is a fantastic choice for Max! Stuffing it with peanut butter-filled Kongs will definitely make it a special treat for him. The combination of the Kong's hollow center and the creamy peanut butter will provide a delightful challenge for Max to figure out how to get the treats out.

Here are some additional tips to make the most out of the Kong Toy:

1. **Start with a small amount:** Begin with a small amount of peanut butter or treats in the Kong to make it easy for Max to get them out. Gradually increase the amount as he becomes more comfortable with the toy.
2. **Mix it up:** Vary the types of treats you use to stuff the Kong. You can try using different flavors of peanut butter, cream cheese, or even canned dog food.
3. **Freeze it:** Freeze the Kong with the peanut butter or treats inside. This will make it more challenging for Max to get them out and provide a fun, cooling treat on a hot day.
4. **Add a twist:** Try adding a twist to the Kong by stuffing it with a few kibbles or small treats, followed by a layer of peanut butter. This will make it more challenging for Max to get to the treats.
5. **Supervise:** Always supervise Max when introducing a new toy, especially one with small parts like the Kong. Make sure he's not trying to chew on the Kong itself or ingest any small pieces.
6. **Rotate toys:** Rotate the Kong Toy with other interactive toys to keep Max engaged and prevent boredom.

Remember, the Kong Toy is not only a great way to keep Max entertained but also helps to reduce anxiety, stress, and destructive behavior caused by boredom.

I hope Max enjoys his new Kong Toy, and you enjoy watching him have fun!
user: I'm thinking of getting Max a new collar with a nice name tag. Do you have any recommendations for a good collar brand or type that would suit a Golden Retriever like Max?
assistant: A new collar with a nice name tag is a great idea! Golden Retrievers like Max deserve a comfortable, durable, and stylish collar that suits their friendly and energetic nature. Here are some recommendations for collar brands and types that would be perfect for Max:

**Collar Brands:**

1. **Yellow Dog Design**: Yellow Dog Design offers a wide range of stylish, comfortable, and durable collars made from high-quality materials like nylon, leather, and cotton. They have a variety of designs, patterns, and colors to choose from.
2. **Puppia**: Puppia is a popular brand that offers soft, comfortable, and adjustable collars made from materials like cotton, polyester, and nylon. They have a wide range of colors, patterns, and designs to suit different breeds and personalities.
3. **Coastal Pet Products**: Coastal Pet Products is a well-known brand that offers high-quality collars made from durable materials like nylon, leather, and rubber. They have a variety of styles, including martingale collars and breakaway collars.
4. **Ruffwear**: Ruffwear is a great brand for outdoor enthusiasts and active dogs like Max. They offer durable, comfortable, and adjustable collars made from materials like nylon, polyester, and rubber.

**Collar Types:**

1. **Nylon Collars**: Nylon collars are a great option for Golden Retrievers like Max. They're durable, easy to clean, and come in a variety of colors and patterns.
2. **Leather Collars**: Leather collars are a stylish option for Golden Retrievers. They're durable, comfortable, and develop a beautiful patina over time.
3. **Breakaway Collars**: Breakaway collars are a great option for dogs that like to play rough or get into trouble. They're designed to release if they get caught on something, reducing the risk of injury.
4. **Martingale Collars**: Martingale collars are a great option for dogs that like to pull or slip out of their collars. They're designed to tighten slightly when the dog pulls, preventing them from slipping out.

**Name Tag Recommendations:**

1. **Engraved Name Tags**: Engraved name tags are a great option for Max. They're durable, easy to read, and can be customized with his name and your contact information.
2. **ID Tags with Rivets**: ID tags with rivets are a stylish option for Golden Retrievers like Max. They're durable, easy to read, and can be customized with his name and your contact information.

When choosing a collar and name tag for Max, make sure to consider the following factors:

* Comfort: Choose a collar that's comfortable and adjustable to fit Max's neck size.
* Durability: Select a collar that's made from durable materials that can withstand Max's active lifestyle.
* Style: Pick a collar that suits Max's personality and your personal style.
* Safety: Consider a breakaway or martingale collar if Max likes to play rough or get into trouble.

I hope these recommendations help you find the perfect collar and name tag for Max!
user: I think I'll go with a nylon collar from Yellow Dog Design. I've heard great things about their products, and they have a wide range of styles and colors to choose from. A nylon collar will be durable and easy to clean, which is perfect for Max's active lifestyle. And, I can get a nice engraved name tag to go with it. Thanks for the recommendations!
assistant: Yellow Dog Design is a fantastic brand, and their nylon collars are a popular choice among dog owners. They're durable, easy to clean, and come in a wide range of styles and colors to suit different breeds and personalities.

A nylon collar will be perfect for Max's active lifestyle, and the engraved name tag will add a touch of elegance and sophistication. It's great that you're considering Max's needs and personality when choosing a collar.

Remember to measure Max's neck size accurately to ensure a comfortable and secure fit. You can also consider adding a little personalized message or Max's name in a fun font to make the collar even more special.

If you have any more questions or need further recommendations, feel free to ask. Otherwise, I hope Max enjoys his new collar and name tag, and you enjoy watching him strut his stuff!

(And don't forget to share pictures of Max in his new collar!)

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:75499fd8

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains several durable user/pet facts and preferences plus useful contextual rationale around dog care, toy choice, medication handling, and collar selection. Forced memory write requires non-noop.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I need help finding a good dog walker in my area. Do you have any recommendations or a list of services that can connect me with a reliable walker?
assistant: Finding a trustworthy dog walker can be a daunting task! Don't worry, I've got you covered. Here are some recommendations and services that can connect you with a reliable dog walker in your area:

**Online Platforms:**

1. **Rover**: Rover is one of the most popular platforms that connects dog owners with local dog walkers and sitters. You can browse profiles, read reviews, and book a walker that fits your needs.
2. **Care.com**: While primarily a childcare platform, Care.com also offers pet care services, including dog walking. You can search for walkers in your area and view their profiles.
3. **Wag!**: Wag! is a dog walking and dog sitting platform that connects you with local walkers. You can book a 30-minute or 1-hour walk, and even track your dog's route in real-time.
4. ** Barkly Pets**: Barkly Pets is a platform that connects dog owners with local dog walkers and sitters. You can browse profiles, read reviews, and book a walker that fits your needs.

**National Dog Walking Services:**

1. **Pet Sitters International**: Pet Sitters International is a network of professional pet sitters and dog walkers across the US. You can search for a walker in your area and contact them directly.
2. **National Association of Professional Pet Sitters**: The NAPPS is a professional organization that connects pet owners with local pet sitters and dog walkers. You can search for a walker in your area and view their profiles.

**Local Referrals:**

1. **Ask Friends, Family, or Neighbors**: Ask people you trust if they know of any reliable dog walkers in your area.
2. **Local Pet Stores or Veterinarians**: Visit local pet stores or veterinarians and ask if they know of any dog walkers they can recommend.
3. **Community Facebook Groups**: Join local Facebook groups or online forums and ask for dog walker recommendations.

**Tips for Finding a Reliable Dog Walker:**

1. **Check Reviews**: Always read reviews from multiple sources to get a sense of the walker's reliability and quality of service.
2. **Conduct an Interview**: Before booking a walker, conduct an interview to discuss your dog's needs, the walker's experience, and their approach to dog walking.
3. **Verify Insurance and Certifications**: Make sure the walker has liability insurance and any relevant certifications (e.g., pet first aid).
4. **Get a Meet-and-Greet**: Meet the walker in person with your dog to ensure they're a good fit.

Remember to always prioritize your dog's safety and well-being when searching for a dog walker. Good luck!
user: I will definitely check out those platforms. Do you think a dog walker would be able to handle Max's flea and tick prevention medication application every two weeks?
assistant: It's great that you're thinking about Max's flea and tick prevention medication. While dog walkers can provide exercise and companionship, administering medication might not be a standard service they offer. However, it's definitely worth asking!

Some dog walkers might be willing to administer flea and tick prevention medication as an additional service, especially if they have experience with dog care or have worked with dogs with similar needs. Others might not feel comfortable or be trained to do so.

Here are a few factors to consider when asking a dog walker about medication administration:

1. **Experience:** Does the dog walker have experience with administering medication to dogs? Have they worked with dogs that require regular medication?
2. **Certifications:** Are they certified in pet first aid or CPR? While not directly related to medication administration, these certifications demonstrate a level of expertise in dog care.
3. **Comfort level:** Is the dog walker comfortable administering medication, and are they willing to take on this responsibility?
4. **Liability:** Who would be liable in case of an adverse reaction or incorrect administration of medication?

If you find a dog walker who is willing to administer Max's flea and tick prevention medication, make sure to:

1. **Provide clear instructions:** Leave detailed instructions on the medication, including dosage, frequency, and any potential side effects.
2. **Leave a schedule:** Create a schedule or calendar to ensure the walker knows when to apply the medication.
3. **Monitor and follow up:** Regularly check in with the walker to confirm the medication has been administered correctly and on schedule.

Alternatively, you could consider other options for medication administration, such as:

1. **Hiring a pet sitter:** Pet sitters often provide more comprehensive care, including medication administration, feeding, and overnight stays.
2. **Asking a trusted neighbor or friend:** If you have a trusted neighbor or friend who is familiar with Max, you could ask them to help with medication administration.
3. **Automated medication dispensers:** There are automated medication dispensers available that can be programmed to dispense medication at set times.

Remember to always prioritize Max's health and safety, and don't hesitate to ask questions or seek guidance from your veterinarian if you're unsure about medication administration.
user: I'm also thinking of getting Max a new toy, something that can keep him engaged during the day while I'm away. Do you have any recommendations for interactive dog toys?
assistant: What a great idea! Interactive dog toys can be a wonderful way to keep Max engaged, stimulated, and happy while you're away. Here are some fantastic options that are sure to delight Max:

1. **Kong Toy**: The Kong is a classic interactive toy that can be stuffed with treats, peanut butter, or even canned dog food. The hollow center provides a challenge for Max to figure out how to get the treats out.
2. **Tricky Treat Ball**: This treat-dispensing ball requires Max to roll it around to release treats. The adjustable difficulty level and varied treat sizes keep the game exciting.
3. **Puzzle Toys**: Puzzle toys, like the Nina Ottosson Dog Brick or the Outward Hound Brick Puzzle, challenge Max to figure out how to get the treats out by sliding, lifting, or rotating the pieces.
4. **Interactive Feeders**: Interactive feeders, such as the Outward Hound Sensory Maze or the Pet Zone Treat Wobbler, provide a fun and challenging way for Max to eat his meals.
5. **Hide-a-Squirrel**: This plush toy has hidden compartments and squeakers, encouraging Max to use his problem-solving skills to find the hidden treats.
6. **West Paw Design's Wobbler**: This weighted, treat-dispensing toy wobbles and moves, making it a fun and challenging way for Max to get his treats.
7. **Sensory Mats**: Sensory mats, like the Snuffle Mat or the Pawfect Pets Sensory Mat, provide a fun and calming way for Max to engage his sense of smell and explore.
8. **Tug Toys with Treats**: Tug toys with hidden treats, such as the Tug-A-Jug or the Ever Pet Interactive Tug Toy, encourage Max to use his problem-solving skills to get the treats out.
9. **Electronic Interactive Toys**: Electronic toys, like the Petcube Bites or the Furbo Interactive Dog Camera, allow you to interact with Max remotely, dispensing treats and playing with him even when you're not there.
10. **DIY Interactive Toys**: You can also create your own interactive toys using household items, such as cardboard boxes, paper bags, and treats. Get creative and have fun!

Remember to choose a toy that suits Max's age, breed, and individual needs. Introduce new toys gradually to prevent overwhelming him, and always supervise playtime to ensure his safety.

Which one of these toys do you think Max would love the most?
user: I think Max would love the Kong Toy. I already know he loves peanut butter, and it would be great to stuff the Kong with peanut butter-filled Kongs as a special treat.
assistant: The Kong Toy is a fantastic choice for Max! Stuffing it with peanut butter-filled Kongs will definitely make it a special treat for him. The combination of the Kong's hollow center and the creamy peanut butter will provide a delightful challenge for Max to figure out how to get the treats out.

Here are some additional tips to make the most out of the Kong Toy:

1. **Start with a small amount:** Begin with a small amount of peanut butter or treats in the Kong to make it easy for Max to get them out. Gradually increase the amount as he becomes more comfortable with the toy.
2. **Mix it up:** Vary the types of treats you use to stuff the Kong. You can try using different flavors of peanut butter, cream cheese, or even canned dog food.
3. **Freeze it:** Freeze the Kong with the peanut butter or treats inside. This will make it more challenging for Max to get them out and provide a fun, cooling treat on a hot day.
4. **Add a twist:** Try adding a twist to the Kong by stuffing it with a few kibbles or small treats, followed by a layer of peanut butter. This will make it more challenging for Max to get to the treats.
5. **Supervise:** Always supervise Max when introducing a new toy, especially one with small parts like the Kong. Make sure he's not trying to chew on the Kong itself or ingest any small pieces.
6. **Rotate toys:** Rotate the Kong Toy with other interactive toys to keep Max engaged and prevent boredom.

Remember, the Kong Toy is not only a great way to keep Max entertained but also helps to reduce anxiety, stress, and destructive behavior caused by boredom.

I hope Max enjoys his new Kong Toy, and you enjoy watching him have fun!
user: I'm thinking of getting Max a new collar with a nice name tag. Do you have any recommendations for a good collar brand or type that would suit a Golden Retriever like Max?
assistant: A new collar with a nice name tag is a great idea! Golden Retrievers like Max deserve a comfortable, durable, and stylish collar that suits their friendly and energetic nature. Here are some recommendations for collar brands and types that would be perfect for Max:

**Collar Brands:**

1. **Yellow Dog Design**: Yellow Dog Design offers a wide range of stylish, comfortable, and durable collars made from high-quality materials like nylon, leather, and cotton. They have a variety of designs, patterns, and colors to choose from.
2. **Puppia**: Puppia is a popular brand that offers soft, comfortable, and adjustable collars made from materials like cotton, polyester, and nylon. They have a wide range of colors, patterns, and designs to suit different breeds and personalities.
3. **Coastal Pet Products**: Coastal Pet Products is a well-known brand that offers high-quality collars made from durable materials like nylon, leather, and rubber. They have a variety of styles, including martingale collars and breakaway collars.
4. **Ruffwear**: Ruffwear is a great brand for outdoor enthusiasts and active dogs like Max. They offer durable, comfortable, and adjustable collars made from materials like nylon, polyester, and rubber.

**Collar Types:**

1. **Nylon Collars**: Nylon collars are a great option for Golden Retrievers like Max. They're durable, easy to clean, and come in a variety of colors and patterns.
2. **Leather Collars**: Leather collars are a stylish option for Golden Retrievers. They're durable, comfortable, and develop a beautiful patina over time.
3. **Breakaway Collars**: Breakaway collars are a great option for dogs that like to play rough or get into trouble. They're designed to release if they get caught on something, reducing the risk of injury.
4. **Martingale Collars**: Martingale collars are a great option for dogs that like to pull or slip out of their collars. They're designed to tighten slightly when the dog pulls, preventing them from slipping out.

**Name Tag Recommendations:**

1. **Engraved Name Tags**: Engraved name tags are a great option for Max. They're durable, easy to read, and can be customized with his name and your contact information.
2. **ID Tags with Rivets**: ID tags with rivets are a stylish option for Golden Retrievers like Max. They're durable, easy to read, and can be customized with his name and your contact information.

When choosing a collar and name tag for Max, make sure to consider the following factors:

* Comfort: Choose a collar that's comfortable and adjustable to fit Max's neck size.
* Durability: Select a collar that's made from durable materials that can withstand Max's active lifestyle.
* Style: Pick a collar that suits Max's personality and your personal style.
* Safety: Consider a breakaway or martingale collar if Max likes to play rough or get into trouble.

I hope these recommendations help you find the perfect collar and name tag for Max!
user: I think I'll go with a nylon collar from Yellow Dog Design. I've heard great things about their products, and they have a wide range of styles and colors to choose from. A nylon collar will be durable and easy to clean, which is perfect for Max's active lifestyle. And, I can get a nice engraved name tag to go with it. Thanks for the recommendations!
assistant: Yellow Dog Design is a fantastic brand, and their nylon collars are a popular choice among dog owners. They're durable, easy to clean, and come in a wide range of styles and colors to suit different breeds and personalities.

A nylon collar will be perfect for Max's active lifestyle, and the engraved name tag will add a touch of elegance and sophistication. It's great that you're considering Max's needs and personality when choosing a collar.

Remember to measure Max's neck size accurately to ensure a comfortable and secure fit. You can also consider adding a little personalized message or Max's name in a fun font to make the collar even more special.

If you have any more questions or need further recommendations, feel free to ask. Otherwise, I hope Max enjoys his new collar and name tag, and you enjoy watching him strut his stuff!

(And don't forget to share pictures of Max in his new collar!)

Vetting emphasis was on reviews, an interview, insurance/certifications, and an in-person meet-and-greet with Max.","tags":["dog_care","service_search","vetting"],"title":"Dog walker search options and vetting criteria for Max","valid_from":"2023-05-22T10:50:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["Max has flea and tick prevention medication applied every two weeks."],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"administering medication might not be a standard service they offer","evidence_reason":"The source directly discusses whether a dog walker can handle Max's biweekly flea and tick medication and gives a vetting/fallback procedure.","importance":4,"keywords":["Max","flea and tick prevention","medication application","dog walker","pet sitter","liability","schedule"],"note_type":"lesson","rationale":"This captures a reusable care-procedure and risk framework, not just the atomic fact that Max has a medication schedule.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Dog-care outsourcing and medication-handling guidance for Max"},"summary":"Max needs flea and tick prevention medication applied every two weeks, but dog walkers may not normally administer medication. If asking a walker to do it, the prior guidance was to verify experience, certifications, comfort level, and liability, then provide clear instructions, a schedule, and follow-up; pet sitters, trusted neighbors/friends, or automated dispensers were alternative options.","tags":["dog_care","medication","outsourcing"],"title":"Medication administration should be specially vetted for Max's dog walker","valid_from":"2023-05-22T10:50:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["Max loves peanut butter.","User planned to get Max a Kong Toy."],"confidence":0.95,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<2>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<7>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I think Max would love the Kong Toy. I already know he loves peanut butter","evidence_reason":"The user explicitly selected the Kong Toy and gave peanut butter as the reason it would suit Max.","importance":4,"keywords":["Max","Kong Toy","peanut butter","interactive dog toy","freeze Kong","rotate toys"],"note_type":"decision","rationale":"This is a choice with rationale and usage context; reducing it to isolated claims would lose why the toy was selected and how it was intended to be used.","scope_json":{"basis":"explicit","kind":"entity","text":"Interactive toy choice and usage rationale for Max"},"summary":"The selected interactive toy direction for Max was a Kong Toy because it can be stuffed with peanut butter, which Max already likes, to keep him engaged while the owner is away. Suggested usage included starting with small amounts, varying fillings, freezing the Kong, adding kibble layers, supervising initial use, and rotating toys to prevent boredom.","tags":["dog_toy","decision","enrichment"],"title":"Kong Toy chosen to keep Max engaged with peanut butter","valid_from":"2023-05-22T10:50:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User planned to get Max a nylon collar from Yellow Dog Design.","User planned to get Max an engraved name tag.","Max is a Golden Retriever."],"confidence":0.96,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<6>","role":"mentioned"},{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I think I'll go with a nylon collar from Yellow Dog Design.","evidence_reason":"The user explicitly chose the brand/type and explained the durability, cleaning, style, and name-tag rationale.","importance":4,"keywords":["Max","Yellow Dog Design","nylon collar","engraved name tag","Golden Retriever","active lifestyle"],"note_type":"decision","rationale":"This is a durable product-selection decision with trade-offs and rationale, not merely a clean atomic preference or ownership fact.","scope_json":{"basis":"explicit","kind":"entity","text":"Collar and name-tag selection for Max"},"summary":"The collar direction for Max was a Yellow Dog Design nylon collar paired with an engraved name tag. The rationale was that Yellow Dog Design has many styles and colors, while nylon is durable and easy to clean for Max's active Golden Retriever lifestyle.","tags":["dog_collar","decision","pet_accessory"],"title":"Yellow Dog Design nylon collar and engraved name tag selected for Max","valid_from":"2023-05-22T10:50:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
